turns.name: JOHN ROBERTS
turns.text: We will hear argument this morning in Case 24-1260, Watson versus Republican National Committee. Mr. Stewart.

turns.name: SCOTT G. STEWART
turns.text: Mr. Chief Justice, and may it please the Court: States have broad power over elections. Throughout our history, they've used that power to change how they hold elections. Nowhere is that change more apparent than in Election Day itself. Congress set the federal Election Day in 1845. At the time, everyone voted in person. There was no absentee voting. There was no secret ballot. Voters challenged each other's qualifications at the polls on Election Day, and states received ballots on Election Day. Over time, states changed all those practices. They did so as Congress extended Election Day to all federal offices. They've done so ever since. No one claims that in setting the federal Election Day Congress blocked most of those changes. The dispute is whether Congress blocked just one change, allowing ballots cast by Election Day to be received after that day. States have allowed that for over a century. Congress has respected it. No one challenged it until now. The question is whether Congress in 1845 blocked that practice. The answer is no. The Election Day statutes adopt a simple rule: States must make a final choice of officers by Election Day. That is the plain meaning of an election. As this Court said in United States versus Classic, from time immemorial, an election to public office has been, in point of substance, no more and no less than the expression by qualified electors of their choice of candidates. Mississippi satisfies that rule. It makes a choice on Election Day. And only that rule respects the last 180 years of state lawmaking. If Election Day must be what it was in 1845, that takes out much more than the ballot receipt laws of 30 states today. It dooms absentee voting, modern methods of voting, the secret ballot, and more. Congress did not adopt that destabilizing view when it simply set the Election Day. The Fifth Circuit was wrong to rule otherwise. This Court should reverse. I welcome the Court's questions.

turns.name: CLARENCE THOMAS
turns.text: Just to be clear, you have -- you said in your opening statement sometimes, you said, the decision -- the choice has to be made by Election Day, and at other points, you say on Election Day. Which is it?

turns.name: SCOTT G. STEWART
turns.text: I -- I think the statewide choice needs to make -- be made by Elect -- or -- or on Election Day itself, when the entire electorate has voted, Justice Thomas. I think voters themselves need -- need to make their individual selections by Election Day.

turns.name: CLARENCE THOMAS
turns.text: If I give my ballot, mail-in ballot, to my neighbor, is that a choice?

turns.name: SCOTT G. STEWART
turns.text: It's a choice. It's not a final choice that can be recognized in the context of an election.

turns.name: CLARENCE THOMAS
turns.text: So when do I know whether or not a choice is final?

turns.name: SCOTT G. STEWART
turns.text: When you've submitted to the state, to the appropriate state election official, through the designated state process.

turns.name: CLARENCE THOMAS
turns.text: So why isn't it the rule then that the final -- or the formalized decision, electoral decision, isn't made until that's done?

turns.name: SCOTT G. STEWART
turns.text: Until -- until it's submitted to the official, Your Honor.

turns.name: CLARENCE THOMAS
turns.text: Yeah.

turns.name: SCOTT G. STEWART
turns.text: I think -- I mean, I think the choice is made once the voter has made -- the voters as -- as a whole have made an irrevocable decision by casting ballots.

turns.name: CLARENCE THOMAS
turns.text: What does that mean?

turns.name: SCOTT G. STEWART
turns.text: A final -- a final choice. They've -- they've cast their ballots.

turns.name: CLARENCE THOMAS
turns.text: Well, if -- I made a final choice when I handed it to my neighbor.

turns.name: SCOTT G. STEWART
turns.text: But it's a final choice within an official state process, Justice Thomas, which --

turns.name: CLARENCE THOMAS
turns.text: Well, I think the ballot is an official state process. Is that enough?

turns.name: SCOTT G. STEWART
turns.text: Not enough to be known in a public way to the state. That happens when it is parted with and submitted through -- through mail to the -- to the appropriate election official. Then it's final.

turns.name: JOHN ROBERTS
turns.text: If -- if "day" means -- includes a period after a particular day of the election, does -- does it include a particular day before the day of the election? Or does your logic require a different consideration?

turns.name: SCOTT G. STEWART
turns.text: Forgive me if I -- I may not be understanding the question correctly, Your Honor, but I think the key is that the Election Day is the day by which a final choice electorate-wide must be made. It can't be made after that day by voters or -- or the electorate as a whole. But individual selections can surely be made before that day because there's still no final choice until you have a -- an electorate-wide deadline.

turns.name: JOHN ROBERTS
turns.text: Is there any limit to that?

turns.name: SCOTT G. STEWART
turns.text: As to how early people could vote?

turns.name: JOHN ROBERTS
turns.text: Sure. Yeah, they fill out a ballot if you're giving them a ballot and, you know, drop it off two weeks before.

turns.name: SCOTT G. STEWART
turns.text: As far as the federal Election Day statutes are concerned, Your Honor, I don't think there -- there is a limit to how early that could occur. I think you just need the day when the electorate as a whole needs to have chosen. There might be other practical or other barriers in that. Of course, elect -- elections are a very long process to begin with, the entire electoral process, so, surely, things are happening weeks and months before. But I think even a couple weeks before, even earlier than that in some cases, people are making individual selections. It's just Election Day itself is the day for the final collective choice.

turns.name: AMY CONEY BARRETT
turns.text: Counsel, I under -- I want to go back to your answers to Justice Thomas. So I understand that Mississippi's particular rule says that it has to be deposited in USPS or with a common carrier, but I don't understand why Mississippi's definition in the next case would preclude a state from adopting a law along the lines of the one that Justice Thomas is proposing. For example, if I have someone in my neighborhood in my HOA who says, listen, I'm going to take everybody's votes in, what if the state said that's fine; if you've cast your final vote and you've -- you know, you've designated someone to carry your vote to -- as long as it gets to the ballot box five days after Election Day, it's fine. Why does your definition preclude what Justice Thomas hypothesized?

turns.name: SCOTT G. STEWART
turns.text: Right. And I think, Justice Barrett, the answer is that submission to mail or common carrier is -- is different in kind than, say, submitting it to a relative or -- or sort of a neighbor in that way.

turns.name: AMY CONEY BARRETT
turns.text: What's the difference? They're not government officials.

turns.name: SCOTT G. STEWART
turns.text: They -- they are impartial third parties that have a duty to deliver what they're owed without altering it.

turns.name: AMY CONEY BARRETT
turns.text: But what about the definition that you're proposing precludes that? Your definition didn't say final as submitted to an impartial third party or final as submitted to a common carrier. You said the final choice has been made. And there are lots of different ways, it seems to me, that you could make a final choice. And you also have the problem of this revocability from the Postal Service regulation.

turns.name: SCOTT G. STEWART
turns.text: So -- so I think, to start with the first part, Your Honor, I mean, I -- I think we're going at a historically recognized form of finality. I think history is -- is helpful in a number of ways, and one way is that as long as we've had mail-in voting, we -- we have -- for almost as long as that time, we -- we've had post-Election Day ballot receipt. I mean, mail-in voting I think in general has been understood as a permissible, unchallenged method of --

turns.name: AMY CONEY BARRETT
turns.text: I wouldn't rely on the history if I were you since you're telling us not to rely on the other side's history, like just because it's always been done that way doesn't mean it has to be done that way, which I actually find as a good point in your favor, but then it seems to me that you're flipping back around and saying, well, absentee voting has always been done that way by common carrier, and so that's how we would expect it to be done.

turns.name: SCOTT G. STEWART
turns.text: Well, I think it says something that for a hundred years we've -- we've had people voting by mail and that's been an understood means of submitting a ballot to the state. I think what it embodies, Justice Barrett, is there's an intuition that when you send -- when you put something through the Postal Service, it's final, it's trusted, it's -- it's not like giving it to kind of a non-official --

turns.name: NEIL GORSUCH
turns.text: Well --

turns.name: SCOTT G. STEWART
turns.text: -- third party.

turns.name: NEIL GORSUCH
turns.text: Well, what -- what if -- what if a state came up with a law that said so long as a notary, that's official, that's recognized, certified that you cast your ballot on Election Day and it was delivered later by whatever means, common carrier or not, what -- what in your theory of the preemptive effect of this statute would preclude that law?

turns.name: SCOTT G. STEWART
turns.text: I think two points on that, Justice Gorsuch. One I think is that --

turns.name: NEIL GORSUCH
turns.text: Pick your best.

turns.name: SCOTT G. STEWART
turns.text: Well, I think one's kind of in -- a point in our favor and one -- one's kind of a point against our friends, which is that there's a finality of submitting it to an official and --

turns.name: NEIL GORSUCH
turns.text: Yeah, it's been -- it's been notarized by -- well, how about a justice of the United States Supreme Court. Is that official enough for you?

turns.name: SCOTT G. STEWART
turns.text: I mean, it -- it -- it's pretty official. I don't think it's been submitted --

turns.name: NEIL GORSUCH
turns.text: It's pretty official.

turns.name: SCOTT G. STEWART
turns.text: It's -- it's pretty good, Your Honor, but I don't -- I don't think it kind of hits submission to the state.

turns.name: NEIL GORSUCH
turns.text: Ah. Okay. Well, that -- I wanted to explore -- so -- so that's not submitted to the state? Even if it's notarized, an -- an affidavit, it was final on the day of and somebody brings it in to the state offices a week or three months later, that doesn't count, but if it goes into the mail, it does? What in the statute -- what words in the statute would you have us read that into, that rule into?

turns.name: SCOTT G. STEWART
turns.text: Well, I mean, I -- I think -- I mean, just to make sure I understand, I -- I think that's fine under my friend's view too, Your Honor. I mean, a notary could be --

turns.name: NEIL GORSUCH
turns.text: I'm asking about your view, counsel.

turns.name: SCOTT G. STEWART
turns.text: Sure. So I -- I think --

turns.name: NEIL GORSUCH
turns.text: You're saying that's not fine, and I want to understand what -- what -- what in the federal law preempts that.

turns.name: SCOTT G. STEWART
turns.text: Well, I think, number one, I -- I just am not -- at least as I'm understanding the hypothetical, it doesn't appear to be submission to the state, Your Honor, in the same --

turns.name: NEIL GORSUCH
turns.text: Oh, it gets submitted to the state three weeks later, just not by common carrier.

turns.name: SCOTT G. STEWART
turns.text: Well, I mean, if -- I mean, if what you're saying, Your Honor, is it's -- that a -- that a notary has been deemed a sufficient ballot receiver under state law, then I think --

turns.name: NEIL GORSUCH
turns.text: Yes. Then that's okay too?

turns.name: NEIL GORSUCH
turns.text: Okay. What if state law -- so now you're saying that is okay.

turns.name: SCOTT G. STEWART
turns.text: I -- I think it -- it may -- you know, it may depend on how -- you know, what the level of officiality is at the time.

turns.name: NEIL GORSUCH
turns.text: Well, the -- the state says it's official, by God, when -- when a Supreme Court justice or -- or perhaps just a notary says it's official, that's good enough under your theory. It has to be, I think. And if that's okay, why can't a state say: How about a time-stamped video showing that I voted on Election Day? Here I am filling out my ballot, and then -- and -- and then my brother or maybe some aggregator of ballots brings it in a week or three later.

turns.name: SCOTT G. STEWART
turns.text: And --

turns.name: NEIL GORSUCH
turns.text: That's got to be okay too, doesn't it?

turns.name: SCOTT G. STEWART
turns.text: I -- I think it's still just -- I -- I -- I keep coming back, Justice Gorsuch, to this pedigree of submitting it to a state. I mean, I think, as a good example --

turns.name: NEIL GORSUCH
turns.text: Well, the state says that's official, the state says that's fine, ballot aggregators are great, and so long as it's a time-stamped video or a notary or maybe a Supreme Court, or who knows what, the librarian says you cast it on -- on the day of the election, we're good -- we're good to go, right?

turns.name: SCOTT G. STEWART
turns.text: I mean, I -- I do -- I do -- do still have some concern that it has not been submitted through --

turns.name: SONIA SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: Counsel, why are you fighting the premise? In the Civil War, at least two states permitted military officers who weren't sworn state officials, they were federal officials but not sworn state officials, to accept the ballots and transmit it by mail to the state, okay? And that was in the Civil War, all right? Since 1918, we've had laws like Mississippi. By 1944, eight of them. By -- since then, 12 more by 1886. And now half the states permit absentee ballots that are dropped in the mail, correct? No different than in the Civil War. We have no federal law that says that's not okay for the state to designate someone by whom an official ballot has to be given by Election Day, correct?

turns.name: SONIA SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: And if the state wants to make it a notary republic, if it wants to make it a military officer, if it wants to make it a Supreme Court justice, if it wants to make it anyone, as long as it's done by Election Day, that's what counts, correct?

turns.name: SCOTT G. STEWART
turns.text: I mean, I think that's possible. We're not quite asking for that, Your Honor. I really don't mean to fight the premise.

turns.name: SONIA SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: No, you don't because you're only defending this law. And this law is very consistent with what happened in the Civil War. It's very consistent with what has happened for over a hundred years. There's nothing in federal law that has prohibited it explicitly, correct?

turns.name: SCOTT G. STEWART
turns.text: That -- that's right, Your Honor.

turns.name: SONIA SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: All right.

turns.name: SAMUEL ALITO
turns.text: Well, Justice Sotomayor is asking you what I think she intends to be a friendly question, but maybe you want to think about whether you want to go that far. What if the state designates in -- an official of the Republican Party or the Democratic Party who receives these ballots, collects ballots, and fills out an affidavit saying, I received all these and -- and I -- I will faithfully deliver them? Would that be okay?

turns.name: SCOTT G. STEWART
turns.text: Well, I think it's -- I think it's actually okay under my friend's view, Your Honor. We're not -- we're not making that ask. I mean, as far as my friend's view are concerned, so long as somebody's designated a ballot receiver, they can be fine. Party boss, party bag man, relative, any of those, so long as the state says you are a ballot receiver, that's fine. The only thing that apparently is not fine is a U.S. Postal Service common carrier. And I think that is, I think, very -- a very odd way to read those statutes --

turns.name: SAMUEL ALITO
turns.text: Well, the Postal Service is not part of the state, and a common carrier isn't part of any government.

turns.name: SCOTT G. STEWART
turns.text: It's -- that's right, Your Honor. But, I mean, certainly, we all know the status of the U.S. Postal Service is a highly regulated government entity with certain obligations. Common carriers in a very similar way, high -- highly regulated with similar obligations. And they give -- in both cases, they're imbued with a duty to deliver what they receive. And, in fact, a duty that's, I -- I think, a good example -- maybe a good example is the fact that the IRS accepts tax returns, which are obviously highly consequential documents with key timeliness requirements, the IRS accepts tax returns both through the mail and through common carriers.

turns.name: SAMUEL ALITO
turns.text: Well, you will -- you will accept ballots that are received within five days after Election Day, that what -- what is, you know, the whatever -- the -- the date that's -- that's set as Election Day, right?

turns.name: SCOTT G. STEWART
turns.text: That's right, Your Honor.

turns.name: SAMUEL ALITO
turns.text: And you have a variety of line-drawing problems. So we've been exploring one, to whom and by what means is this thing to be -- the ballot to be delivered to the state. But how -- what -- what about the length of time that a state could choose? Is it the case -- isn't it the case that some state allows -- will -- will count ballots that are received 21 days after Election Day?

turns.name: SCOTT G. STEWART
turns.text: There are a couple that at the outlier it's kind of canvassing and it can go that far. I think more common is about 310 --

turns.name: SAMUEL ALITO
turns.text: Well, is that okay? Is that all right?

turns.name: SCOTT G. STEWART
turns.text: As far as -- as far as the federal Election Day statutes are concerned, yes, Your Honor. And I think this actually gets to a good point that -- that loops back to something Justice Gorsuch was mentioning, was -- was getting at in a certain way, which I think he was saying, one, the federal Election Day statutes decides that. I -- I think that's the key point in our favor, is this -- this is an area where states get to go first to make these decisions. Congress can step in, but the idea --

turns.name: SAMUEL ALITO
turns.text: So there's no limit? Except I suppose the day when the presidential electors have to be appointed or the day when the -- the next Congress begins, starts its session, that's the only limit on counting -- on counting mail-in ballots?

turns.name: SCOTT G. STEWART
turns.text: Those are some of the limits, Your Honor. But I'd also say the federal Election Day statutes don't require states to count ballots at all. It doesn't speak to counting, period. So, I mean, if a state really didn't want to --

turns.name: NEIL GORSUCH
turns.text: Not -- not counting but -- but being delivered. So I do think it is a consequence, and you can tell me if I'm wrong, but just so we can wrap this up, that by any means and by any date up until the next Congress meets, a state can receive ballots?

turns.name: SCOTT G. STEWART
turns.text: I think, for congressional races, potentially, yes, Your Honor. I think there -- there are other Title III kind of things that push action earlier in the presidential context, but, again, I mean, there's nothing -- the key question is these are one -- three one-sentence provisions, and we want to be, I think, very careful not to read them for more than they're worth.

turns.name: HOWELL JACKSON
turns.text: And, counsel, isn't --

turns.name: SONIA SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: Counsel --

turns.name: HOWELL JACKSON
turns.text: I was just going to say, isn't -- isn't your point that the line-drawing issues that have been raised are only problems to the extent that Congress thought they were problems? We're -- we're in a preemption dynamic, and so the question I think really is, what did Congress intend with its statement about Election Day? Did it mean to cabin the states so that they did not have the discretion to make these kinds of decisions? And, in addition to what Justice Sotomayor has said, which is we have no federal statute that precludes this, I think we have several stat -- federal statutes that suggest that Congress was aware of post-Election Day ballot deadlines that the states had enacted and, in fact, incorporated those in several circumstances. So can you speak to that? I'm -- I'm talking about UOCAVA, for example, and some other federal statutes that indicate that Congress not only knew this was going on, but then it interpreted and incorporated the state's own post-ballot -- post-Election Day ballot deadlines into federal law.

turns.name: SCOTT G. STEWART
turns.text: Sure, Your Honor. So I -- I -- I think that's exactly right. So UOCAVA is -- is maybe the best example where Congress was told that about a dozen or so states have post-Election Day ballot receipt laws. It respected those explicitly as to a deadline for -- for ballots in -- in -- in that law. I think the United States has agreed with us on -- on that very point.

turns.name: HOWELL JACKSON
turns.text: And Congress wasn't worried, I think, about the states picking dates that were, you know, we might think are too long or whatnot. That -- that was not in the statute. Congress just said whatever the state has decided with regard to ballot receipt deadlines is going to apply here, correct?

turns.name: SCOTT G. STEWART
turns.text: Right. And that's been the case for 180 years, Your Honor. And I think one feature that I -- you know, obviously, we're -- we're -- we're searching for what did the statutes mean at the time, but I think it's very hard for my -- my friends to explain a hundred -- the past hundred years of history and this lawmaking. The Soldier Voting Act in the 1940s was -- was another example where Congress recognized and respected state deadlines. And it just -- it gets very hard to explain those things if you don't adopt the view that the federal Election Day statutes just do not set a ballot receipt deadline.

turns.name: NEIL GORSUCH
turns.text: I want to ask you about the recall problem, and before I get to that, throughout your brief, you say that the -- that the federal statute does require voters to submit their ballots to election officials on Election Day, must be cast by Election Day, and that the -- and that the Election Day is the day to conclude and consummate the election through a final selection. You agree with all those statements in your brief?

turns.name: SCOTT G. STEWART
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: NEIL GORSUCH
turns.text: Okay. But, at the same time, you say: Actually, it doesn't have to be submitted to an election official; it just has to be submitted to a common carrier. And there's a contradiction there that I -- I just want you to first address, and then I'll give you my hypothetical.

turns.name: SCOTT G. STEWART
turns.text: Very good, Your Honor. I think, when you put something in the mail, you're not -- I think --

turns.name: NEIL GORSUCH
turns.text: That's not an election official. FedEx isn't an election official.

turns.name: SCOTT G. STEWART
turns.text: Right, but the recipient certainly is the person who you're submitting -- submitting it in the mail to. I mean, that is -- that is the recipient.

turns.name: NEIL GORSUCH
turns.text: Sure. Of course.

turns.name: SCOTT G. STEWART
turns.text: And, right, Your Honor. And I think --

turns.name: NEIL GORSUCH
turns.text: You submit it to FedEx and they deliver it to the election official, but -- but you say it has to be submitted to an election official throughout your brief, but then you say a common carrier is okay, and that -- those two things don't add up.

turns.name: SCOTT G. STEWART
turns.text: Well, I think the difference is, say, you know, sending it to your brother versus sending it to the registrar.

turns.name: NEIL GORSUCH
turns.text: Well, we already dealt with that. The brother turns out to be okay so long as the state says so. You --

turns.name: SCOTT G. STEWART
turns.text: I think that's okay on their view, Your Honor.

turns.name: NEIL GORSUCH
turns.text: It's also okay --

turns.name: SCOTT G. STEWART
turns.text: I -- I'm a little more -- more guarded than that.

turns.name: NEIL GORSUCH
turns.text: Well, you -- I think you already answered that one. So -- so here's the hypo. Let's say you have a state where a large portion of the electorate mails in their ballots on or close to Election Day. Not far-fetched. Many states are like that. Then the day after the election, a story breaks that one of the lead candidates engaged in an inappropriate sexual escapade or perhaps is concluding with a foreign power. Again, not far-fetched, I think. And the competing candidate immediately goes on the airwaves and urges voters to recall their ballots and -- and -- and to tell the common carriers not to deliver them. And many common carriers will do that with anything that you send through the -- through them. FedEx, you just call them up and say I want it back. In that hypothetical, did the election happen on Election Day? Oh, by the way, it swings the election.

turns.name: SCOTT G. STEWART
turns.text: So the -- the election did happen on -- on Election Day, Justice Gorsuch. As -- as we've explained, our ballot does not allow using mail recall, anything like that. When somebody submits their ballot by mail, it's final.

turns.name: NEIL GORSUCH
turns.text: Well, just -- just first deal with my hypothetical, and then I'll deal with your statute.

turns.name: SCOTT G. STEWART
turns.text: Okay. The -- the -- the election happened on Election Day. It sounds like --

turns.name: NEIL GORSUCH
turns.text: Even though it changes the outcome?

turns.name: SCOTT G. STEWART
turns.text: I mean, I think it --

turns.name: NEIL GORSUCH
turns.text: Yes has to be your answer, doesn't it?

turns.name: SCOTT G. STEWART
turns.text: I mean -- I mean, yes, Your Honor. I'm just --

turns.name: NEIL GORSUCH
turns.text: Okay.

turns.name: SCOTT G. STEWART
turns.text: I -- I -- I want to be clear about the --

turns.name: NEIL GORSUCH
turns.text: All right. And then --

turns.name: SCOTT G. STEWART
turns.text: -- we are not agreeing that the outcome can be properly changed in that circumstance.

turns.name: NEIL GORSUCH
turns.text: Well, hold on. It did in my circum- -- in my hypothetical. You can't change my hypothetical, counsel.

turns.name: SCOTT G. STEWART
turns.text: And I'm just saying it's unlawful, Your Honor.

turns.name: NEIL GORSUCH
turns.text: It's un -- what's -- what's unlaw- -- what --

turns.name: SCOTT G. STEWART
turns.text: I don't mean to disparage your hypothetical. I'm just saying that it -- that it's an unlawful circumstance because we don't allow ballot -- ballot recall in our state.

turns.name: NEIL GORSUCH
turns.text: Oh, in Mississippi, okay. But -- but that hypothetical could happen in another state, right?

turns.name: SCOTT G. STEWART
turns.text: I think, if the state is not providing that on mailing there's a final choice made, I think that would be a problem with that law under the federal statutes, Your Honor.

turns.name: NEIL GORSUCH
turns.text: Okay. Now you say your statute -- you're -- you're -- you're different. You admit my hypothetical could happen, but you say it can't happen in Mississippi because recall's not allowed. I couldn't find that anywhere in Mississippi law. In fact, what I did see was a statute that says that you -- that the Secretary of State can promulgate rules and regulations. That's 23-15-637(3). And then I went and looked at the regulations, and Rule 2.1 says that an absentee ballot is the final vote of a voter when the ballot is marked accepted. That doesn't preclude recall.

turns.name: SCOTT G. STEWART
turns.text: And --

turns.name: NEIL GORSUCH
turns.text: In fact, that allows recall.

turns.name: SCOTT G. STEWART
turns.text: -- I -- I don't -- I respectfully don't agree with that, Your Honor. I mean, what --

turns.name: NEIL GORSUCH
turns.text: Where -- where does it say recall is not permitted? I couldn't find that anywhere in your statutes or the rules.

turns.name: SCOTT G. STEWART
turns.text: And I think, by providing that ballots are final when cast under our statute itself --

turns.name: NEIL GORSUCH
turns.text: No, it doesn't. It says they're final when marked accepted.

turns.name: SCOTT G. STEWART
turns.text: That's the regulation, Your Honor.

turns.name: NEIL GORSUCH
turns.text: Yeah. It's your --

turns.name: SCOTT G. STEWART
turns.text: Yeah. And it -- and --

turns.name: NEIL GORSUCH
turns.text: It's your regulation, and it allows recall.

turns.name: SCOTT G. STEWART
turns.text: It does -- respectfully, it does not allow recall, Your Honor. The ballot is final when cast.

turns.name: NEIL GORSUCH
turns.text: Show me where it -- would you read to me the provision that precludes it?

turns.name: SCOTT G. STEWART
turns.text: It's -- it's subsection (3) of the statute that my friends have challenged.

turns.name: NEIL GORSUCH
turns.text: I read you subsection (3) that says the Secretary of State can make rules.

turns.name: SCOTT G. STEWART
turns.text: And --

turns.name: NEIL GORSUCH
turns.text: And then I read you the rule.

turns.name: SCOTT G. STEWART
turns.text: To ensure that the ballots are final when cast. They -- the -- the key thing --

turns.name: NEIL GORSUCH
turns.text: But the rule says --

turns.name: SCOTT G. STEWART
turns.text: -- is that ballots are final when cast.

turns.name: NEIL GORSUCH
turns.text: It says -- no. Votes promulgated by an absentee ballot with the absentee ballot -- person's absentee vote is final. You can make rules about when they're final, and what the rule says it is final when marked accepted.

turns.name: SCOTT G. STEWART
turns.text: And that's -- I mean, it's speaking to a processing rule about what to do when somebody's -- you know, when somebody's ballot potentially doesn't arrive on time. They submit an affidavit ballot. But I would come back to the -- the text of the statute, Your Honor. I mean, "final when cast" is -- is what our law --

turns.name: AMY CONEY BARRETT
turns.text: Mr. Stewart, I just want to ask a clarifying point to this. Is it possible for a portion of Mississippi's statute to be unlawful, not -- just -- just go with me here -- a portion of Mississippi's statute to be unlawful insofar as it potentially permits recall, but still that, you know, would not address whether Mississippi's statute was unlawful insofar as it allowed ballot receipt after Election Day?

turns.name: SCOTT G. STEWART
turns.text: Yes, Your Honor. I mean, I think one possibility would be, like, hey, look, you need a more unambiguous finality here and you need to foreclose recall, period, full stop.

turns.name: AMY CONEY BARRETT
turns.text: Okay. And -- and on this question, I just want to make sure I understand what you're saying. When you said, yes, my friends on the other side say it's okay for private parties to deliver just like you do, are you saying that's just a manner of receipt, and whether your -- whether the ballot is received before or after the election, on all sides, everybody agrees that absentee ballots can be turned in by having, you know, the neighbor, the party operative, or whatever scoop them up and bring them to the -- the ballot box?

turns.name: SCOTT G. STEWART
turns.text: Your Honor, I mean, maybe -- you know, I'm not sure if I answered the earlier thing as crisply as I could. I'm not comfortable with the neighbor piece. I do have serious concerns about that. I -- I --

turns.name: AMY CONEY BARRETT
turns.text: But is that tied to the ballot receipt problem, or is that just tied to, hey, maybe this is an unlawful way of executing absentee balloting regardless of when the ballots make it to the official?

turns.name: SCOTT G. STEWART
turns.text: I think it's the finality of the choice that has to be done by Election Day. And --

turns.name: AMY CONEY BARRETT
turns.text: Okay.

turns.name: SCOTT G. STEWART
turns.text: -- my -- my concern is that if you're not sending it by mail or common carrier --

turns.name: SONIA SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: You're getting caught up, counsel, again. Could you go back to the question? Justice Barrett is not arguing with you.

turns.name: SCOTT G. STEWART
turns.text: Fair, Your Honor.

turns.name: SONIA SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: What she's saying and what you said earlier is -- and you believe that under your adversary's -- the opposing counsel's theory, you can give it to the RNC operative, you can give it to anybody, and so long as it's received on Election Day by the state, that's okay. That's what you're saying your opposing counsel's saying. And you're saying the only question before us is, if they can designate an appropriate vehicle to transmit the votes, then that's okay.

turns.name: SCOTT G. STEWART
turns.text: I think I was saying the lat -- the latter part, yes, Your Honor.

turns.name: SONIA SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: Mm-hmm.

turns.name: SCOTT G. STEWART
turns.text: I actually think, under my friend's view, it's okay for -- like, a state can deem the ballot -- deem received when the party operative himself or herself receives it because all that person has to do is be a ballot receiver designated by the state.

turns.name: SONIA SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: Right.

turns.name: SCOTT G. STEWART
turns.text: And we're trying to hew more closely not just to the text but also the historical hundred-year-long you can submit it by mail.

turns.name: SONIA SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: Okay.

turns.name: JOHN ROBERTS
turns.text: Thank you --

turns.name: SONIA SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: So -- but the bottom line is that states can choose when the final vote has to be counted?

turns.name: SCOTT G. STEWART
turns.text: States have discretion over counting.

turns.name: SONIA SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: Exactly.

turns.name: JOHN ROBERTS
turns.text: Thank you, counsel. Justice Thomas, anything further?

turns.name: CLARENCE THOMAS
turns.text: Just as a point of clarification, without looking at the subsequent history, what do you think the federal law required as far as the Election Day?

turns.name: SCOTT G. STEWART
turns.text: That the voters make a final choice by that day. The -- the way I'd boil it down, Justice Thomas, is choice is critical and unchanging; method of making that choice is malleable. And that does also explain all the subsequent history.

turns.name: CLARENCE THOMAS
turns.text: And you don't have to formalize that choice?

turns.name: SCOTT G. STEWART
turns.text: You do by submit -- by parting with your ballot and submitting it to the state.

turns.name: SONIA SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: Have we had any --

turns.name: JOHN ROBERTS
turns.text: Justice -- Justice --

turns.name: SONIA SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: I'm sorry.

turns.name: JOHN ROBERTS
turns.text: -- Alito?

turns.name: SAMUEL ALITO
turns.text: Your position does require some difficult line-drawing problems, and maybe that's inevitable, but one is the degree of confidence that one must have in the entity or person who transmits the ballot to the state. We've talked about that. One is how long after the Election Day vote -- votes must be -- votes can be received. A third is whether it has to be postmarked. Are there -- aren't there quite a -- aren't there eight states that do not require a postmark for late ballots?

turns.name: SCOTT G. STEWART
turns.text: There are a number of states that don't require that, Your Honor. I think postmarking is -- is good evidence that something has been timely submitted. That's kind of borne out in the tax context. I -- I think, you know, if -- if somebody were challenging a law like that, it would potentially be a very different kind of a challenge. They'd have to make sort of factual showings to challenge that ballots were not cast by Election Day but surely not this case.

turns.name: SAMUEL ALITO
turns.text: Well, maybe these -- maybe it's inevitable that some sort of line-drawing decisions like these have to be made, unless the rule is anything goes, you know, states can do anything they want in this area. We don't have a whole lot to go on here. We have the phrase "Election Day" and we have history. If we looked just at the phrase "Election Day," what would we take from that? I think you're -- you've been saying we're -- and we're moving in this direction. We don't have Election Day anymore. We have election month or we have election months. I mean, the -- the early voting can start a month before the election. The ballots can be received a month after the election.

turns.name: SCOTT G. STEWART
turns.text: Well, I -- I think the best way to read it, Your Honor, is that by setting Election Day, the Congress set the final choice day. And that's the key thing, final choice day. What Congress was concerned about at the time was some states making final choices, say, a month before other states were making final choices and the distortions, the fraud, the risks that -- that that had. And when everybody's making a final choice on the same day, even if some individual choices are made before then, you're honoring the statutes, and I think -- you know, I think, Justice Alito, you made this observation, you know, not a lot to go on because the statutes are just not very -- there's not a lot of prolixity there. I think that's a point in favor of deference to the states. You know, if Congress has not spoken to something, especially in this context, where states are expressly empowered to regulate these things, that's a big point in favor of letting states continue to do so as they -- they have for a century.

turns.name: SAMUEL ALITO
turns.text: Do you think it's legitimate for us to take into account Congress's desire -- Congress's passage of the Election Day statutes for the purpose of combatting fraud or the appearance of fraud and will and some of the briefs have argued that confidence in election outcomes can be seriously undermined if the apparent outcome of the election at -- on the day after the polls close is radically flipped by the acceptance later of a big stash of -- of ballots that flip the election or -- yeah.

turns.name: SCOTT G. STEWART
turns.text: I think --

turns.name: SAMUEL ALITO
turns.text: You've got ballots that are delivered by somebody -- by -- you know, by a neighbor and they're received a month after the election and they don't have postmarks.

turns.name: SCOTT G. STEWART
turns.text: I -- I think the best way to do it is to honor the statute's purpose by -- but doing so by respecting the statute's text and animating context. And what these statutes were getting at was not just any kind of fraud writ large in all elections, which, you know, no statute could do, but a situation where, okay, you know, State 1 votes -- you know, has their election on one day, ends it, and State 2 neighboring has its election three weeks later because the concern would be let's say State 1 goes for one party and then State 3 goes for the other party with a huge turnout for that other party. You could have potential fraud. People went across state lines and voted, or you could have the appearance of that sort of fraud even if it didn't actually happen, like people just said, oh, my gosh, they voted for that person, I -- you know, big turnout. So I think that's the kind of thing that animated those statutes, whereas fraud writ large was not -- was not a good example. I do think it's -- it's notable that my -- my friends with the United States, you know -- you know, obviously, they -- they -- they've sounded the antifraud theme. They haven't cited a single example of fraud from post-Election Day ballot receipt in this century.

turns.name: SAMUEL ALITO
turns.text: All right. Okay. Thank you. I'll let my colleagues have a chance.

turns.name: SCOTT G. STEWART
turns.text: Thank you.

turns.name: JOHN ROBERTS
turns.text: Justice Sotomayor?

turns.name: SONIA SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: Has there been any history of voter recall in the mails in -- in Mississippi? You say the law doesn't permit it, but has there been any history of it?

turns.name: SCOTT G. STEWART
turns.text: None that I've ever heard of, Your Honor. I mean, this isn't something that was raised until the appellate reply briefs. And nobody cited a single example in history of it.

turns.name: SONIA SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: All right. And I understand Justice Alito's questions about the policy questions of what will create discomfort or not. But the Constitution vests the issue of elections in the states unless superseded by Congress, correct?

turns.name: SCOTT G. STEWART
turns.text: That's right, Your Honor.

turns.name: SONIA SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: So, if there is a policy question to be had, the entities to decide that are the states and Congress, not the courts, correct?

turns.name: SCOTT G. STEWART
turns.text: That's right, Your Honor.

turns.name: SONIA SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: And absent a law in federal law that suggests that absentee ballots must be received by a certain time, there's no explicit requirement of that, correct?

turns.name: SCOTT G. STEWART
turns.text: Right, Your Honor, or even implicit.

turns.name: SONIA SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: And -- and the implicit, we have the military acts that since World War II at least and the Civil War -- I should go back that far -- have permitted absentee ballots to be received long past Election Day, correct?

turns.name: SCOTT G. STEWART
turns.text: We have a very long history of that, Your Honor.

turns.name: SONIA SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: Exactly. So the people who should decide this issue are not the courts but Congress, correct?

turns.name: SCOTT G. STEWART
turns.text: Right, Your Honor. And I -- I think what --

turns.name: SONIA SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: The states and Congress.

turns.name: SCOTT G. STEWART
turns.text: That's right, Your Honor. And as I -- as I was mentioning in my colloquy with, I believe, Justice Alito earlier, that's kind of the structure of our system. States go first. If Congress doesn't like it, it overrides. And, as I think Justice Scalia said for the Court in Arizona Inter Tribal, we respect what Congress said as far as it went but no farther. And that's kind of the history of voting and election law in the country to begin with.

turns.name: SONIA SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: One last question. With respect to history, history is informative of what's been permitted by Congress because what we're looking for is what understanding Congress had with respect to elections, correct?

turns.name: SCOTT G. STEWART
turns.text: That's right, Your Honor, yeah.

turns.name: SONIA SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: All right. And it doesn't talk about the receipt of absentee ballots after elections at all, so we look to the history of whether Congress has accepted it, correct?

turns.name: SCOTT G. STEWART
turns.text: I think that does help here, Your Honor.

turns.name: SONIA SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: All right. But the attempt by some of the amici here to Bruenize this inquiry and say that history tells us how elections must be held and on what date and when receipt can happen or not happen, why is that not an acceptable way to look at this?

turns.name: SCOTT G. STEWART
turns.text: Well, I -- I think the difference between Bruen and here, Your Honor, is that Bruen -- in Bruen, the Court -- the -- the question at issue, you know, whether a restriction on firearms is consistent with our historical tradition of firearm regulation, there's a rich history addressing precisely that issue, sometimes through analogy, but very much, I mean, Bruen itself, I believe, I want to say 40 pages of history just analyzing those things. We don't have anything like that, a rich history suggesting that Congress was even thinking about locking in Election Day ballot receipt or anything like it. It was addressing different things.

turns.name: SONIA SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: All right. Thank you, counsel.

turns.name: SCOTT G. STEWART
turns.text: Thank you, Your Honor.

turns.name: JOHN ROBERTS
turns.text: Justice Kagan?

turns.name: ELENA KAGAN
turns.text: I guess, Mr. Stewart, your answers to Justice Sotomayor's questions leave me unsure about what you think about these appeals to history. I mean, you've said that there's not a lot of text here. I agree with that. I think that the way this case has been argued all around suggests that that's so. So -- so then we're left with history. Or are we left with history? You seem to appeal to history sometimes -- Justice Barrett said this -- but then repudiate the appeals to history at other times. And I guess I want to know what we're supposed to be doing when we're looking at this statute and deciding this question with respect to all this historical evidence, scant or not scant as it may be.

turns.name: SCOTT G. STEWART
turns.text: Sure, Your Honor. So I -- I -- I think the best way I can break it down is this, is that history's informative here, and the way it's most informative is that since Congress set -- I mean, throughout our history and, certainly, since Congress set the federal Election Day, election administration in our country has been very, very dynamic. As I tried to highlight in my opener, it was one thing, in-person, on-the-spot qualification checks by fellow voters, ballots were naturally received on Election Day, all those features. As soon as Congress had a reason to alter the methods by which a choice was made, it did so. But the one constant is that the voters, in a popular government, it's most critically the voters, the constant is that the voters make a final choice of officers by Election Day. So I think history shows that election administration is dynamic. States have wide leeway. They just have to make sure that the voters make a choice by Election Day.

turns.name: ELENA KAGAN
turns.text: Thank you.

turns.name: SCOTT G. STEWART
turns.text: Thank you, Your Honor.

turns.name: JOHN ROBERTS
turns.text: Justice Gorsuch?

turns.name: NEIL GORSUCH
turns.text: Almost done, Mr. Stewart. You -- you emphasized to Justice Kagan and many others that the -- the final choice at least has to be done on Election Day and so recall would be a problem for you if -- if it could happen. But you say that -- that that doesn't happen in Mississippi, that that's -- that's your position, right?

turns.name: SCOTT G. STEWART
turns.text: Right. It's not permitted.

turns.name: NEIL GORSUCH
turns.text: If recall could happen, that would be a problem for your position?

turns.name: SCOTT G. STEWART
turns.text: I think there -- potentially. I think there's a --

turns.name: NEIL GORSUCH
turns.text: Because then it wouldn't be a final decision on Election Day, right?

turns.name: SCOTT G. STEWART
turns.text: That's right, Your Honor.

turns.name: NEIL GORSUCH
turns.text: Okay.

turns.name: SCOTT G. STEWART
turns.text: I think there would still be a factual question, though, of whether -- I still have not been cited anything to say that recall is actually a thing that can happen in the rule.

turns.name: NEIL GORSUCH
turns.text: No, I understand. But, if it could happen, that would be a problem for your theory because the final choice wouldn't have happened on Election Day, right?

turns.name: SCOTT G. STEWART
turns.text: Fair, yeah.

turns.name: NEIL GORSUCH
turns.text: Okay. So you -- you say Mississippi prohibits it. We -- we've -- we've been through whether that's the case or not.

turns.name: SCOTT G. STEWART
turns.text: Very good.

turns.name: NEIL GORSUCH
turns.text: But let -- let's say it did. Let's say there was a law prohibiting recall. How would it ever be enforced? Because how is a state to know and how is even a common carrier carrying an envelope to know whether that is a ballot that needs to be recalled? And who are you going to prosecute?

turns.name: SCOTT G. STEWART
turns.text: Well, I think -- I think it could be enforced similar to -- to other vote -- voter things. I mean, in our state, there are notarization and affidavit requirements, signing things. I think one -- if -- if -- if it were critical to -- for the voter to say that there will be no recall, the voter could say, I hereby commit that I will not attempt to or recall this package in any way, shape, or form. They could just add that, sign it. I take the point about proof problems, but, I mean, gosh, Justice Gorsuch, I mean, violating state election laws in a state that really is antifraud and really takes wide measures to enforce its laws, that would be quite a thing for a voter to do.

turns.name: NEIL GORSUCH
turns.text: Well, in my hypothetical, which, you know, you say is unlikely to swing an election and all of that on recall, but as soon -- you know, if -- if history teaches anything, scant or not scant, it's that as soon as something's allowed, it will happen eventually, right? And, you know, so -- so somebody -- my hypothetical happens and everybody recalls their ballots. I'm just not sure what recourse the state would have against people who violated its anti-recall law.

turns.name: SCOTT G. STEWART
turns.text: I mean, if people are, say, recalling things through a common carrier, there's going to be some record that they recalled those things. A state could --

turns.name: NEIL GORSUCH
turns.text: How's a state ever going to find out about it?

turns.name: SCOTT G. STEWART
turns.text: I mean, I think the same -- the same way it would find --

turns.name: NEIL GORSUCH
turns.text: It's going to go -- you're going to go prosecute individuals?

turns.name: SCOTT G. STEWART
turns.text: I mean, Your Honor, I'd say, like, if somebody is lying in an affidavit that they're not going to recall something, I mean, if -- if people are doing that with impunity, we have quite a problem, but it is a problem that the state can address and enforce --

turns.name: NEIL GORSUCH
turns.text: I just don't know how they'd ever find out about it.

turns.name: SCOTT G. STEWART
turns.text: I mean, I think you -- obviously, you know, some crimes are harder to find out and prosecute than others, Your Honor, but I think it's still -- I mean, people can do things, try to, like, tamper with ballots, all that sort of thing. I think it's just still a matter of respecting the statute.

turns.name: NEIL GORSUCH
turns.text: Thank you.

turns.name: SCOTT G. STEWART
turns.text: Thank you, Justice Gorsuch.

turns.name: JOHN ROBERTS
turns.text: Justice Kavanaugh?

turns.name: BRETT KAVANAUGH
turns.text: When did Mississippi switch to this method?

turns.name: SCOTT G. STEWART
turns.text: 2020. In 2024, it added common carriers.

turns.name: BRETT KAVANAUGH
turns.text: And why?

turns.name: SCOTT G. STEWART
turns.text: It was first because of the pandemic. I have -- I don't know that there's necessarily an answer about why as to the 2024 one in a clear -- in a clear way.

turns.name: BRETT KAVANAUGH
turns.text: And the other side makes a point when we're looking at history, to the extent historical practice is relevant, that it only became widespread to allow it to be mailed by Election Day in more recent years and that the predominant approach was to require receipt by Election Day throughout the historical practice you cite, until very recently. How are we supposed to think about that?

turns.name: SCOTT G. STEWART
turns.text: I mean, I think it's the same way to think about, I -- I think, the broader history of election law, is, when -- when states have kind of seen a problem, they've adapted and adjusted in -- often in -- in the direction of allow -- making sure more votes can be counted. I mean, that was obviously a big thing when absentee voting started to become a prominent thing. You know, certainly, we cared for much about our history of people showing up in person, but we realized that was very difficult for people away from home and that we wanted to, you know, kind of widen the tent and make it -- make it so that people could vote. And I think perhaps another realization, even though we've had UOCAVA for 40 years, Justice Kavanaugh, is that for military voters in particular, this is a perennial challenge about making sure that they can actually get their ballots in on time. I mean, that's perhaps why UOCAVA voters in particular are a very popular group for post-Election Day ballot receipt.

turns.name: BRETT KAVANAUGH
turns.text: Would you say that the states that require receipt by Election Day are disenfranchising voters?

turns.name: SCOTT G. STEWART
turns.text: No, you're not -- they're not, Your Honor. I mean, a reasonable ballot deadline does not do that. I would asterisk just there are the practical barriers for those overseas military voters.

turns.name: BRETT KAVANAUGH
turns.text: But, for the citizens who are not within that class, you would not use the term "disenfranchisement" to say it has to be received by Election Day, November 3rd, rather than -- I guess it would be five business days -- November 10th? You would not use that term to describe that, correct?

turns.name: SCOTT G. STEWART
turns.text: I would not. Correct, Your Honor.

turns.name: BRETT KAVANAUGH
turns.text: And then picking up on Justice Alito's questions, Professor Pildes and others have said that late-arriving ballots open up a risk of what might destabilize the election results. "If the apparent winner the morning after the election ends up losing due to late-arriving ballots, charges of a rigged election could explode. The longer after Election Day any significant changes in vote totals take place, the greater the risk that the losing side will cry that the election has been stolen." And my question is -- my questions, one, is that a real concern? Two, does that factor into how we think about how to resolve the scant text and the maybe conflicting or evolving history here?

turns.name: SCOTT G. STEWART
turns.text: I think -- I certainly respect the perception. I think one thing notable in this case and I think helpful is that there -- there -- there has not been much -- much of a showing about actual fraud from post-Election Day ballot receipt itself. I mean, that was --

turns.name: BRETT KAVANAUGH
turns.text: Well, I think Justice Alito referred to and I think this quote refers to the appearance of fraud. And is that a real concern? Is that something we should be thinking about, confidence in the election process? Just -- just curious how we factor that in here.

turns.name: SCOTT G. STEWART
turns.text: Right, and I think it would go back to the same answer I had for Justice Alito about scant -- you know, there -- simple text, straightforward text, and respecting the limits of that text, and looking at the animated history, I think it really was -- it was the double-voting fraud concern. I don't know that people have that particular concern. I think, you know, obviously, people can be, you know, unhappy when a -- a result flips. I think Congress may be able to take measures to address that. But I think there's no good evidence that Congress was doing that beyond preventing double-voting in this context.

turns.name: BRETT KAVANAUGH
turns.text: But, if you have to have a deadline, which you acknowledged, and if having a deadline of November 3rd rather than November 10th doesn't -- for receipt, doesn't disenfranchise anyone, why wouldn't it make more sense to -- to take account in some respect of that concern as we think about how the text and history fit together?

turns.name: SCOTT G. STEWART
turns.text: Well, I think, I mean, something that Congress was clearly, I think, trying to do was adopting a simple rule in this text. And I think a very simple rule is everybody must cast their ballot by Election Day. I mean, that's quite administrable. I mean, if you have an Election Day ballot receipt deadline, there are certainly things to commend that. I think you're alluding to some of those, Justice Kavanaugh. But there's also a question of, when do I vote? You know, I'm not sure. You know, do I need to do it 7, you know, whatever it is? Whereas, you know, if somebody wants to get all the information possible, they like voting on Election Day, you have a pretty nice, clear, simple rule, and that seems to dovetail quite well with the --

turns.name: BRETT KAVANAUGH
turns.text: Well, the simple rule, but, if it arrives on November 11th, you're out.

turns.name: SCOTT G. STEWART
turns.text: Right, but I think that's kind of baked into the risks you always get when you're doing -- there are always some risks, you know, regrettably with mail-in voting. You can eliminate some of those risks by voting in person, but --

turns.name: BRETT KAVANAUGH
turns.text: The risks would seem the same no matter whether it's November 3rd or November 10th, in other words, that it could take longer than a week for the mail to get there.

turns.name: SCOTT G. STEWART
turns.text: The risk is there, but I -- I think, if you have Election Day ballot receipt -- and I -- I think what we're addressing, I think, are more -- the way I might frame the issues are these are policy points. The question is, did Congress wall off states from debating these policy points? I think the answer is no. And I think states could very reasonably say, you know, look, we -- we do want to get counting and get a result very fast, but we are concerned about these military voters, we do want voters to be able to get whatever -- whatever information they want. I mean, the weekend before, you know, that used to be a big deal in elections at least. Maybe it still could be in the future. And I think a state could say: Hey, look, federal law didn't wall us off from doing this. We get that it has some things about it that other states might weigh differently, but we want to allow that for our voters.

turns.name: BRETT KAVANAUGH
turns.text: Well, I take your point, but it really goes to just thinking about what Election Day or day of election means. But I -- I take your point. Thank you.

turns.name: SCOTT G. STEWART
turns.text: Thank you, Your Honor.

turns.name: JOHN ROBERTS
turns.text: Justice Barrett?

turns.name: AMY CONEY BARRETT
turns.text: So I want to be clear about what your definition of finality requires. Would it require finality if the vote arrives by Election Day as well? So let's -- let's imagine an absentee ballot that's put in the mail or sent by common carrier well in advance of Election Day and does -- is received and put in the ballot box by Election Day. Does that one have to be final? Or, in other words, would it be a problem for you that the U.S. Postal -- Postal Service permits recall for those ballots that are sent in advance? I guess what I'm asking is, do you have the same problem either way? You've -- you've taken a lot of questions, and I have those questions, about what the effect of this recall ability is. And I guess my question is, I want to understand what your definition of finality is and whether it applies to the earlier absentee ballots as well. Do they have to be final such that it would be illegal to recall them pursuant to whatever regulation the post office has?

turns.name: SCOTT G. STEWART
turns.text: Right. I think I got it, Justice Barrett. Let me see if I can do my -- my best on it. I mean, I think, in theory, if Election Day has not passed, there could be a window for somebody to --

turns.name: AMY CONEY BARRETT
turns.text: So it would be okay? It wouldn't be illegal for somebody to recall a ballot before Election Day? I guess I don't understand why --

turns.name: SCOTT G. STEWART
turns.text: Well, I mean, if a state allowed that, Your Honor. I mean, we don't -- we don't allow it.

turns.name: AMY CONEY BARRETT
turns.text: So it would be okay for the state -- I know you don't allow it.

turns.name: SCOTT G. STEWART
turns.text: Right.

turns.name: AMY CONEY BARRETT
turns.text: But I guess what I'm under -- I guess I'm struggling because, if your definition of the election or the vote or casting the vote requires finality, I'm trying to understand why that would be different before the election -- when I'm saying before the election, I'm talking about receipt -- for earlier-sent absentee ballots and for later-sent. It seems like the same rule should apply regardless. But it seems like you're not -- you're saying something different.

turns.name: SCOTT G. STEWART
turns.text: Yeah, I -- I -- I'm having a -- I guess I'm having a hard time conceiving kind of a dual-track rule in -- in -- in that circumstance.

turns.name: AMY CONEY BARRETT
turns.text: So would it be -- let me try to put it this way. Would it be illegal for a state to permit absentee voting but permit recall or -- or just kind of be silent about this Postal Service regulation that permits recall or anything a common carrier did to permit recall? Is recall a problem even if it's a possibility for those earlier absentee ballots? Because you can't consider them final. All these questions that you've been taking are about whether you could really consider the ballot to be final if you could recall it. So I'm asking whether that same finality test applies for the earlier ballots.

turns.name: SCOTT G. STEWART
turns.text: And I'm not sure, Your Honor. I mean, I think -- I -- I guess I just keep coming back to you can't recall or change your vote or -- or make any effort like that --

turns.name: AMY CONEY BARRETT
turns.text: And that's true no matter when you send it.

turns.name: SCOTT G. STEWART
turns.text: I think, I mean, certainly, in our state, and I think -- I think that's right, Your Honor.

turns.name: AMY CONEY BARRETT
turns.text: But I mean as a matter of the federal law. It just seems to me that your definition has to be consistent, and it sounds like you're changing it for the earlier absentee ballots and saying finality has some kind of different force for the later-received absentee ballots.

turns.name: SCOTT G. STEWART
turns.text: Right. Well, I -- I think a state has to do whatever it would need to do to guarantee that on Election Day, all ballots -- you know, every -- every cast vote is final and cannot be recalled. I -- I -- I'm having --

turns.name: AMY CONEY BARRETT
turns.text: But it doesn't have to be final at the time it's sent because it's okay to recall it. If you send it two weeks in advance and you change your mind, that's not a problem.

turns.name: AMY CONEY BARRETT
turns.text: Okay. I'll withdraw.

turns.name: SCOTT G. STEWART
turns.text: Yeah, I guess I'm having trouble seeing how --

turns.name: AMY CONEY BARRETT
turns.text: Okay.

turns.name: SCOTT G. STEWART
turns.text: -- the federal Election Day statute itself would necessarily allow that, Your Honor.

turns.name: AMY CONEY BARRETT
turns.text: Well, it's about your definition of election. It's about your definition of what it means to cast a vote, but let's put that aside. Obviously, there's a lot of talk about history in this case. Well, the Electoral Count Reform Act was passed in 2022, and for the first time, it defined Election Day. Should that matter? So should we be looking at how Congress understood Election Day to mean in the period of voting, what it thought in 2022, or should we be looking at the late 19th century and the Civil War practices?

turns.name: SCOTT G. STEWART
turns.text: I think most vitally it still remains 1845 is the key date, especially given that we have statutes that are trying to manifestly achieve uniformity and what is required of all federal offices. So I -- I do think 1845 is the key date. And I --

turns.name: AMY CONEY BARRETT
turns.text: So Congress carried over in 2022 the same understanding of Election Day that had persisted throughout?

turns.name: SCOTT G. STEWART
turns.text: I think that's right, final choice of an officer.

turns.name: AMY CONEY BARRETT
turns.text: Okay. Thank you.

turns.name: SCOTT G. STEWART
turns.text: Thank you, Your Honor.

turns.name: JOHN ROBERTS
turns.text: Justice Jackson?

turns.name: HOWELL JACKSON
turns.text: So let me just clarify. This case is not about a Mississippi practice or policy related to recalling ballots, is that right?

turns.name: SCOTT G. STEWART
turns.text: That's right, Your Honor.

turns.name: HOWELL JACKSON
turns.text: So I -- I guess I'm a little confused about those kinds of policy questions, and I guess -- I think that the Constitution's allocation of responsibility here actually makes this case about who decides. I mean, there are a lot of policy questions. Justice Alito ran through several of them, who gets to receive the ballot, how long after can they be submitted, does it have to be postmarked. I suppose we can add is recall allowed, et cetera, et cetera. But the question, I think, is whether Congress has precluded the states from making those calls, drawing those lines, and your position as I understand it is no.

turns.name: SCOTT G. STEWART
turns.text: That's correct, Your Honor.

turns.name: HOWELL JACKSON
turns.text: That the -- that the scantness of Election Day in the federal statutes actually is a point in your favor because it indicates that Congress was leaving it to the states to draw the various lines that might arise in this circumstance. Is that right?

turns.name: SCOTT G. STEWART
turns.text: That's right, Your Honor. And that's reinforced, for example, by -- by the 1866 statute, which shows that when Congress wanted to be really prescriptive in this area, it was very prescriptive. It described this is what the states have to do and so forth.

turns.name: HOWELL JACKSON
turns.text: So, if we were focused, really focused on what we're asked to answer here, which is, is the -- are the states preempted by federal law from having post-Election Day ballot deadlines, you would say no?

turns.name: SCOTT G. STEWART
turns.text: No. No. Congress just didn't decide that.

turns.name: HOWELL JACKSON
turns.text: Let me ask you about the history with respect to Bruen. I -- I -- I guess I'm not sure I understood your answer to Justice Sotomayor because it seems to me that Bruen is not even the right methodology by which to be thinking about this. Bruen applied to the Second Amendment because the Second Amendment incorporated a preexisting right. And the point of the history and tradition test was to try to evaluate the contours of that right. We -- we don't have that dynamic here. We're trying, I thought, to figure out what Congress meant when it included Election Day in its federal statutes. So it seems odd to me that the suggestion that the limitation on our understanding of Congress's intent is somehow tied to the state's practices.

turns.name: SCOTT G. STEWART
turns.text: I think that's right, Your Honor. I think the other thing -- I think the point I'd emphasize is that the idea of embodying a preexisting right confirms that there's something very much unchanging. And the thing that we know about election administration, it has very much been changing throughout the course of our country.

turns.name: HOWELL JACKSON
turns.text: It's been changing throughout the course in a way that undermines the notion that there was one consistent practice, first of all, or that Congress's law, the meaning of Election Day in the federal statutes, somehow was tied to what the states' practices were about that. I mean, I think this was your original point, that there were lots of different practices. And so, to the extent we're saying, oh, you could only look at Election Day in the federal statute to mean exactly what the states were doing back at that time, this imperils a lot of different things, not just post-Election Day ballot deadlines, right?

turns.name: SCOTT G. STEWART
turns.text: That's right, Your Honor. And, I mean, the only way to explain all of these changes is to read them in line with plain text and the limits of history, which is to say election is a final choice. I mean, otherwise, you have to do what my -- my friends have to do, which is, hey, look, we're going to pick out the one practice from these eight -- 19th century codes that we like, ballot receipts, say that's what an election is, and just ignore in-person voting, qualification check on the spot by fellow voters, and that's just not sound history.

turns.name: HOWELL JACKSON
turns.text: Can I ask you one final thing about the amicus brief from the Society For the Rule of Law Institute, because I found it very interesting, and I didn't really know what your view of it was. That amicus brief focused on the Electoral College and Congress's setting it up, you know, back in 1787 with the understanding that the casting of votes can happen on a particular day and the receipt of those votes by election officials can happen on a different day. And, in fact, the way the Electoral College is set up, I think, at the time, Congress permitted in nine -- in 1792 about a month to elapse between the casting of votes, which, by the way, it called Election Day, and the receipt of the votes by the -- the electors submitted them to the president of the Senate up to a month after. So I -- I think that to the extent we're worried or thinking about historical evidence, that seems to me to be pretty significant and compelling historical evidence of Congress's understanding of what was required by Election Day versus the receipt of those ballots at some subsequent point, correct?

turns.name: SCOTT G. STEWART
turns.text: Yeah. I think that's -- that's well stated, Your Honor. The -- the other thing I'd tie it to in the same amicus brief, and I think the -- the brief, the amicus brief does a nice job of tying it to this, is that the key thing is -- is choice, choosing. The Constitution refers to choosing, choice, you know, those -- those -- those kind of verbs. And that's why that sort of thing is allowed, that the key thing is --

turns.name: HOWELL JACKSON
turns.text: And the choice is made when you cast your vote under the electoral process system, when the electors cast their vote, right?

turns.name: SCOTT G. STEWART
turns.text: Right.

turns.name: HOWELL JACKSON
turns.text: And that is distinguished -- I'm just trying to make clear that the casting of the vote and the receiving of the votes historically have been conceived of as two different things that can actually happen at different times, right?

turns.name: SCOTT G. STEWART
turns.text: I think so, Your Honor.

turns.name: HOWELL JACKSON
turns.text: Thank you.

turns.name: SCOTT G. STEWART
turns.text: Thank you, Your Honor.

turns.name: JOHN ROBERTS
turns.text: Thank you, counsel. Mr. Clement.

turns.name: PAUL D. CLEMENT
turns.text: Mr. Chief Justice, and may it please the Court: All agree that elections for federal office have to end on the day of the election specified by Congress, and all agree that you can't have an election unless you receive ballots, and there must be some deadline for ballot receipt. Nonetheless, Mississippi insists that ballots can trickle in days or even weeks after Election Day. That position is wrong as a matter of text, precedent, history, and common sense. Mississippi all but concedes that the original public meaning of election included both offering to vote and the receipt of that vote or ballot by election officials. And, of course, the key distinction between voting and an election is an election involves the combined action of voters and election officials, as this Court underscored in its decision against Foster against Love. And, of course, Mississippi insists that at the time these statutes were passed, ballot receipt and the -- the casting of the ballot were so inextricably intertwined no one would have thought of one without the other. That seems to me to be a damning admission, but it also ignores the advent of field and proxy voting in the Civil War and the enormous efforts that states went to to ensure that all of the ballots, whether by proxy or by field vote, were received by Election Day. In the state's view, all of those herculean efforts were for naught or were entirely gratuitous. Now the state's position actually works even worse as a matter of common sense. If somebody in Gulfport the day after the election asks is the election over, the common sense answer is no, it's not. The ballots are still coming in. And if somebody asks who won, the truthful answer is we don't know why yet. The ballots are still coming in, and they may trickle in for weeks or months. And, in fact, they may trickle in for weeks or months with or without a postmark in differing ways in differing states. That reality gives the lie to the idea that we have a uniform national Election Day. I welcome the Court's questions.

turns.name: CLARENCE THOMAS
turns.text: How would you define the day of election?

turns.name: PAUL D. CLEMENT
turns.text: I would say that the day of the election is the day when -- it's the last day in which all the ballots are cast and they are received into official custody.

turns.name: CLARENCE THOMAS
turns.text: So how would you treat early voting as compared to late reception of votes?

turns.name: PAUL D. CLEMENT
turns.text: So I would say, I mean, you know, I think the best place to look for a treatment of early voting is the Kreisling -- the Keisling decision by Judge Kleinfeld in the Ninth Circuit because, after Foster against Love, there was a suggestion in Foster against Love that maybe early voting is a problem. He rejected that claim based on two things. One is the distinct history of early voting, and the second is the idea that was explicit in this Court's decision in Foster against Love that Election Day is the date of consummation. So I would say, under our theory, early voting is permissible largely because it has a different history and because of this idea that the Election Day is the date where the election is consummated.

turns.name: CLARENCE THOMAS
turns.text: Would you spend a few more minutes on or at least a little bit more time on the voting during Civil War? There was some suggestion that that's an example of late reception of votes. And I think, in your intro, and this was my thinking, that it was not, that proxy voting was a way to make sure that the vote occurred on Election Day as opposed to afterwards.

turns.name: PAUL D. CLEMENT
turns.text: Well, not surprisingly, Justice Thomas, you are exactly right. So proxy voting is the thing that happened in the Civil War that is most analogous to absentee voting. And the thing that is most striking is -- I think five states had proxy voting -- every one of those five states required the votes, the ballots, to be received by election officials back home by Election Day. Now that's an incredibly inconvenient thing that was done in the Civil War to ensure the ballots were received by Election Day. And under the state's view, they didn't need to do that. Now it's really no different in the context of field voting because there were, like, maybe a dozen states -- if you're not going to count the Confederate states, there were about a dozen states that did field voting. Again, every one of those ensured that the ballots were received into official custody by Election Day. And, of course, most of the states went to enormous efforts to replicate the machinery of the ballot box and everything else in the field and -- but -- and some variation in that. But the one thing that didn't vary at all was that the ballots had to be received into official custody by Election Day. And just one last thought on this Civil War practice. My friend's only real answer to the idea that, at 1845, there was an understanding that everybody would have had that, of course, the election involves both the offer to vote and receiving the vote, the ballot, into official custody, his only answer to that is, well, like, nobody -- the issue hadn't ripened, that nobody had even thought that you could divide the two. But the issue ripened in the Civil War, and the issue ripened in such a way that every state required the ballot to be received into official custody by Election Day. And then that certainly informs what Congress is thinking in 1872 when they extend the presidential Election Day deadline to the congressional realm.

turns.name: SONIA SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: Mr. Clement, what do I do with the two states during the Civil War, Rhode Island and Nevada? You say five favor you. At least two don't. Nevada simply designated three of the highest officers in command, didn't swear them in, just said three officers in the command can create a ballot box or suitable receptacle for votes and collect the votes of the soldiers with little further formality. Those military officers then would mail those ballots in and they would be counted, whether they arrived on Election Day or after. What do we do with Rhode Island, that simply directed soldiers to deliver a written or printed ballot with the name of the person voted for to the officer commanding the regiment to which he belonged? That officer wasn't sworn in as a state official either, and receipt could happen thereafter. So it's not -- yes, many states believed, as they did at the time, that voting had to be in person, that voting had to be not -- it had to be by voice instead of by paper. Lots of states had lots of beliefs about what a proper election should look on. But how do we deal with the outliers? Or you just want to ignore them?

turns.name: PAUL D. CLEMENT
turns.text: No, I don't want to ignore the outliers. I actually think Rhode Island and Nevada help me and my side of this case, and I think that's because they did designate somebody -- they might not have given them an oath, but they designated somebody as an official to receive the boats -- the votes. And they had to come in --

turns.name: SONIA SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: So there -- here, what's the difference between the post office and a common carrier? It's the official.

turns.name: PAUL D. CLEMENT
turns.text: No.

turns.name: SONIA SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: You want an individual person, is that it?

turns.name: PAUL D. CLEMENT
turns.text: Well, I -- I want somebody who's cloaked with government authority, so that's one difference. But there's a critical second difference.

turns.name: SONIA SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: But it's not state authority, meaning you're now expanding what you're saying as government authority.

turns.name: PAUL D. CLEMENT
turns.text: So what I would say is it was government authority, and I would say that my understanding of the history is that all those federal officers voluntarily accepted the responsibility, whether they took an oath or not. And I think that's quite --

turns.name: SONIA SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: So could a neighbor and so could anybody else. I'm not moved. The point is that it permitted someone else to deliver the item, a final cast vote, to deliver it and be received after. Under your theory of this case -- and I think you and the government disagree on this -- you believe that absentee voting by the military and overseas voters, the various laws, that federal laws under which states have proceeded with respect to those votes are illegal?

turns.name: PAUL D. CLEMENT
turns.text: No, I don't --

turns.name: SONIA SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: Or -- or -- or they're not saying what everybody has understood them to say, that states can accept absentee ballots after the election?

turns.name: PAUL D. CLEMENT
turns.text: So I --

turns.name: SONIA SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: Military and overseas ballots?

turns.name: PAUL D. CLEMENT
